chapters.chapter_summary: The patient was positioned supine with a shoulder roll elevating the right shoulder and the head turned to the left. General anesthesia was administered, and the surgical site was marked with lines indicating the planned incision.
A circular bone flap was elevated using a single burr hole, exposing the relaxed dura. The dura was opened, and the tumor was identified near the primary sensory cortex.
The tumor was carefully dissected from the surrounding brain tissue using various instruments. The tumor was removed in one piece, and sodium fluorescein was used to identify any residual tumor.
Hemostasis was achieved using bipolar cautery and other hemostatic agents. The surgical site was inspected for any residual tumor or bleeding.
The dura was re-approximated and duroplasty was completed. The bone flap was re-approximated, and synthetic materials were used to cover the border between the bone flap and cranium.
Postoperative MRI scans showed no radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. The patient had no neurological deficit and was discharged home.

SOAP.Subjective: I performed a surgical resection of a large mass at the temporoparietal junction in a 20-plus year old patient who presented with several weeks of headache, progressive weakness on the left side of the body, and difficulty with gait and balance.
SOAP.Objective: I identified the tumor near the primary sensory cortex and performed a dissection using computer navigation and neurophysiological monitoring. I observed the tumor being removed in one piece, and sodium fluorescein was used to identify any residual tumor. Hemostasis was achieved, and the surgical site was inspected for any bleeding or residual tumor.
SOAP.Assessment: I assessed the procedure as successful with the tumor removed in a gross total fashion. No neurological deficit was observed postoperatively.
SOAP.Plan: I closed the wound in layers and used synthetic materials to achieve a better cosmetic result. The patient was discharged home with no neurological deficit. Postoperative MRI scans showed no residual or recurrent tumor.
